title: Memory-assisted multimode microwave-to-optical transduction
authors: Ujjwal Gautam, Nasser Gohari Kamel, Sourabh Kumar, Daniel Oblak
abstract: Microwave-to-optical quantum transducers will enable coherent interconnection between distant superconducting quantum devices. Ongoing explorations with several platforms have shown promising results at single-photon levels. However, in all these demonstrations, elimination of noise due to the concurrence of the weak transduced signal with intense pump pulses remains a challenge, requiring high suppression filtering setups. A memory-assisted transducer, on the other hand, offers a versatile approach that not only mitigates the noise but also enables the on-demand retrieval of the transduced signal. Here, we integrate a quantum memory protocol with transduction in a three-level atomic system to demonstrate on-demand retrieval of transduced signals. Due to the zero-first-order Zeeman transitions at zero magnetic fields, providing long optical and spin coherence times, and GHz range hyperfine splitting, we use a low-doping concentration $^{171}{\rm Yb}^{3+}$:${\rm Y}_2{\rm SiO}_5$ crystal at 30\,mK temperature. We achieve on-demand transduction assisted by memory with $0.4\ (\text{and }0.3)$ noise photons in the detection window at a storage duration of $460\ (\text{and }620) \, μ\textrm{s}$. To demonstrate the coherent nature of the protocol, we show interference patterns resulting from transduced signals due to varying phase or frequency of the input microwave pulses. Further, multimode transduction capacity is demonstrated, utilizing the spin and optical inhomogeneous broadening. The on-demand capability of the protocol allows synchronizing qubits in a quantum repeater protocol, while multimode capacity increases the entanglement generation rate. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of an on-demand microwave-to-optical transducer assisted by memory.
summary: The researchers have demonstrated the first microwave-to-optical transducer that uses a quantum memory to enable on-demand signal retrieval. By using a Yb-doped crystal, they successfully reduced noise from pump pulses and showed that multiple modes can be transduced simultaneously. This advancement is crucial for building scalable quantum networks connecting superconducting processors.
main_problem: The difficulty of eliminating noise caused by intense pump pulses during microwave-to-optical transduction, which is essential for connecting superconducting quantum devices.
main_result: Demonstration of the first on-demand microwave-to-optical transducer assisted by a quantum memory, achieving low noise levels (0.3-0.4 photons) and multimode transduction capacity.
method: Integration of a quantum memory protocol with transduction within a three-level atomic system using a rare-earth-ion-doped crystal.
model_or_system: A low-doping concentration 171Yb3+:Y2SiO5 crystal operated at a temperature of 30 mK, utilizing zero-first-order Zeeman transitions and GHz-range hyperfine splitting.
key_observables: Noise photon counts in the detection window, storage duration, interference patterns from varying input microwave pulse phase/frequency, and multimode transduction capacity.
important_parameters: Storage durations of 460 and 620 microseconds; noise levels of 0.4 and 0.3 photons; 30 mK operating temperature.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of noise in transduction, describes the implementation of a memory-assisted protocol in Yb-doped crystals, demonstrates on-demand retrieval and coherence via interference, and concludes with the demonstration of multimode capacity.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for quantum optics and quantum networks as it provides a solution for synchronizing qubits in quantum repeater protocols and increases entanglement generation rates through multimode capacity.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
